2557.description: The nearest major galaxy to the Milky Way — a barred spiral containing ~1 trillion stars. Visible to the naked eye on moonless nights. On a collision course with our galaxy; they may merge in ~4 billion years.

2555.description: A compact elliptical dwarf satellite of Andromeda. One of the brightest companions of M31, discovered by Guillaume Le Gentil in 1749.

2429.description: Dwarf elliptical satellite of Andromeda with a peculiar elongated shape. Contains dust lanes unusual for ellipticals, hinting at past interactions.

5818.description: The third-largest member of the Local Group. A face-on spiral with loose arms and vigorous star formation — one of the most distant objects visible to the naked eye.

3082.description: A dwarf irregular galaxy and satellite of the Milky Way. Visible from the southern hemisphere, it was used by Henrietta Leavitt to discover the period-luminosity relation of Cepheids.

17223.description: The Milky Way's largest satellite — a barred Magellanic spiral. Home to the Tarantula Nebula, the most active star-forming region in the Local Group.

5974.description: A grand-design spiral often called the Phantom for its low surface brightness. Host to multiple supernovae, making it a key calibrator for the distance ladder.

28630.description: A grand-design spiral and dominant member of the M81 Group. Its bright nucleus and well-defined arms make it a classic example of the Hubble sequence.

28655.description: A starburst galaxy shaped like a cigar, tilted edge-on. Gravitational encounters with M81 triggered a massive burst of star formation — a nearby laboratory for understanding distant starbursts.

42407.description: One of the most photographed galaxies — a spiral with a brilliant bulge and a dark dust lane that gives it a sombrero profile. Host to ~2,000 globular clusters.

44182.description: A spiral with a dramatic dark band of dust across its bright nucleus — the 'black eye.' Formed by a merger; the inner and outer regions rotate in opposite directions.

47404.description: The prototypical grand-design spiral — two sweeping arms winding from a bright core. Interacting with NGC 5195; the encounter has amplified its spiral structure.

47413.description: The Whirlpool's companion — a peculiar barred lenticular. Its gravitational pull has distorted M51's arms and triggered enhanced star formation in the Whirlpool.

42038.description: A classic edge-on spiral — a thin needle of light. Its prominent dust lane bisects the disk, revealing the structure of spiral galaxies seen from the side.

34695.description: One of the Leo Triplet — a barred spiral with tightly wound arms. Interacting with M66 and NGC 3628; the trio offers a snapshot of galaxy group dynamics.

40455.description: A giant elliptical in the Virgo Cluster and part of Markarian's Chain. Its smooth, featureless appearance typifies old stellar populations in dense cluster environments.

41361.description: One of the 'Eyes' — a barred lenticular paired with NGC 4438. Part of the striking Markarian's Chain, a line of galaxies in the Virgo Cluster.

37229.description: One half of the famous Antennae — a pair of colliding galaxies. The merger has triggered a firestorm of star formation and produced long tidal tails of ejected stars.

37230.description: The other half of the Antennae merger. Together with NGC 4038, it offers a preview of the Milky Way–Andromeda collision billions of years from now.

2248.description: A spectacular ring galaxy formed when a smaller galaxy punched through its disk ~200 million years ago. The collision sent a density wave outward, creating the iconic cartwheel shape.

39225.description: The nearest radio galaxy — a peculiar lenticular with a dramatic dust lane. A past merger left a supermassive black hole that powers enormous radio lobes.

41377.description: A supergiant elliptical and the dominant galaxy of the Virgo Cluster. Host to the first black hole ever imaged — 6.5 billion solar masses at the center of a galaxy.
